name: Copper Globe and other mines in San Rafael district
short_description: Explore historic mining sites in the San Rafael district where vibrant malachite and chrysocolla specimens occur in copper mineralization. Open BLM land with no permit required for recreational col...
description:
The Copper Globe and associated mines in the San Rafael district represent some of Utah's most historically significant copper workings. Located on public BLM land in Emery County, this area offers rockhounders the chance to search for secondary copper minerals including malachite and chrysocolla that formed from oxidized copper ore bodies. The district saw active mining interest from the late 1800s through mid-1900s, and abandoned prospects and mine dumps still yield quality specimens for patient collectors.

The geology here reflects the typical Basin and Range structure common to eastern Utah, with mineralization related to Tertiary intrusive activity and subsequent supergene enrichment processes. Copper-bearing veins oxidized near the surface to produce the green and blue minerals that attract rockhounders today. The area's exposed geology and accessible mine workings make it an excellent location for understanding copper mineralization processes.

Collecting at Copper Globe requires exploration of old mine dumps, tailings piles, and surrounding mineralized outcrop. You may find malachite in botryoidal or banded forms, often mixed with chrysocolla and limonite. Specimens range from small hand samples to larger chunks suitable for display or lapidary work. The remoteness of the location means you will likely have sites largely to yourself, though the terrain is moderately challenging.

Always approach abandoned mine structures with extreme caution - never enter mine portals or underground workings due to cave-in hazard and poor air quality. Collect only from surface dumps and exposed outcrop. The San Rafael district remains a favorite destination for serious specimen collectors seeking authentic mining-area finds with real geological provenance.
history: The San Rafael mining district was developed during the copper boom of the late 1800s and early 1900s, with the Copper Globe mine being one of several producers in the area. Copper mineralization here resulted from Tertiary-age igneous intrusion and associated hydrothermal activity that deposited primary copper sulfides, which were later oxidized near the surface to form the secondary malachite and chrysocolla minerals visible today.
tips: Focus your search on old mine dumps and tailings piles where oxidized copper minerals are concentrated - these discolored earth mounds are visible from distance and mark productive collecting spots. Bring a crack hammer and chisel to work specimen-bearing rock; malachite often occurs as thin veneers on host rock and requires careful extraction. Look for bright green banding or botryoidal textures paired with blue chrysocolla; the contrast between the two minerals on a single specimen is highly prized. Avoid any enclosed mine workings entirely - collect only from surface exposures where you can see daylight.
terrain_notes: High desert terrain at roughly 5,500-6,000 feet elevation with sparse vegetation including juniper and pinyon pine. The landscape is rocky and broken with numerous washes and ridges. Terrain around old mine dumps is uneven with loose talus and sharp mineralized rock fragments. Distances from parking to productive areas range from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on which prospects you target. Footing is rough in places; sturdy boots with ankle support are essential.
best_season: April - October. Spring and fall offer mild temperatures ideal for exploring remote areas. Summer heat (80-90F) is manageable at this elevation but water sources are scarce. Winter snow and cold make access difficult from November through March.
written_directions: From Castle Valley, head east on State Route 24 toward the San Rafael Swell. Continue approximately 15 miles and look for mining-related signs or turnoffs. The exact access point varies by which specific mine or prospect you target; topographic maps and GPS coordinates are essential. Road conditions become progressively rougher - high-clearance vehicle strongly recommended for final approach. Consult detailed mineral guides or local BLM office for current access information to specific sites.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road with potential washboard, high clearance required. Final approaches to mine sites may require 4x4 in wet conditions.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts near mine sites. No developed parking facilities. Space for 2-4 vehicles at most locations.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Do not enter mine portals or underground workings. Collect only from surface dumps and exposed outcrop. Pack out all trash.

nearest_services: Castle Valley, approximately 15 miles northwest. Limited services; stock up on water and supplies before departing town.
accessibility_notes: Uneven, rocky terrain with loose talus and sharp mineralized fragments. Rough footing throughout, no wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support required. Multiple mine portals present serious hazard - must maintain safe distance. Moderate hiking distances of up to 2 miles. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you find at Copper Globe mines in Utah?
faq.answer: The primary minerals you will find are malachite (bright green, often banded) and chrysocolla (blue to blue-green). These secondary copper minerals formed from oxidized copper ore bodies. Specimens often display beautiful color contrast between green and blue minerals on single pieces. Limonite (iron oxide) is also common on mine dumps.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at the Copper Globe mines in Emery County?
faq.answer: No permit is required. The Copper Globe area sits on public BLM land where recreational mineral collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You do not need to contact the BLM in advance, but you must follow collection rules: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation.

faq.question: How do you find malachite and chrysocolla specimens at San Rafael district mine sites?
faq.answer: Focus on old mine dumps and tailings piles where oxidized copper minerals concentrate. Look for discolored earth mounds visible from distance - these mark productive areas. Use a crack hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from host rock. Malachite often occurs as thin veneers that require delicate work. Search exposed outcrop and surface material only - never dig in underground workings.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Copper Globe mines in Utah?
faq.answer: The ideal season is April through October when temperatures are mild at this 5,500-6,000 foot elevation. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable conditions for exploring remote areas and working mine dumps. Summer (June-August) is possible but can reach 80-90F with minimal water sources. Winter snow and cold from November through March make access difficult.

faq.question: What tools and vehicle do you need to collect at the Copper Globe area?
faq.answer: Bring a crack hammer, chisel, and sturdy hiking boots with ankle support for rough, rocky terrain. A high-clearance or AWD vehicle is strongly recommended for rough dirt roads with washboard conditions. Final approaches to some mine sites may require 4x4 in wet weather. Always pack extra water - the remote desert location offers no reliable sources.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Copper Globe mines beginner-friendly or suitable for families?
faq.answer: This location is best suited for experienced rockhounders age 12 and up due to rough terrain, loose talus, sharp mineralized rock, and serious hazards from abandoned mine structures. Never enter mine portals - the risk of cave-in and poor air quality is extreme. Beginners should gain experience at more accessible sites first. Families with older, capable children can visit with careful supervision and strict adherence to safety rules.

meta_description: Find malachite and chrysocolla at Copper Globe mines in Utah's San Rafael district. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit required. Moderate difficulty.
